_comment: Per-chart narratives. CI (build_prompt.py -> GitHub Models -> write_narrative.py) overwrites these from live numbers; this committed copy is the fallback so the page always reads well. source = 'fallback' | 'github-models'.

notes.fossil: As of June 2026, WTI crude oil is priced at approximately $79.2 per barrel, Brent crude at $81.62 per barrel, and US regular gasoline at $3.85 per gallon. Henry Hub natural gas stands at $2.83 per MMBtu, reflecting its inherent volatility compared to oil and gasoline. Indexed to 2019 levels, natural gas has exhibited the most extreme fluctuations, spiking sharply in 2022 before retreating, while crude oil and gasoline prices have moved more closely together. This contrast highlights natural gas's sensitivity to supply-demand dynamics and seasonal factors.
notes.transition: The levelised cost of electricity, which represents the average cost to produce one megawatt-hour (MWh) of electricity over the lifetime of a facility, shows that solar PV costs $44/MWh and onshore wind costs $33/MWh globally. Both these renewable sources now undercut the cost of new coal-fired generation at $110/MWh and combined cycle gas turbines (CCGT) at $76/MWh. Solar PV's levelised cost has declined approximately 90% since 2010, from around $460/MWh to $44/MWh, indicating rapid technological improvements and cost efficiencies in solar power deployment.
notes.currency: The estimated share of global oil trade settled outside the US dollar is about 20% and increasing, though no official data exists for this figure. Despite this gradual shift, the US dollar retains dominant status in oil trade invoicing and settlements. This drift is mainly driven by Russia's pivot towards Asian buyers and an increase in transactions settled in yuan and rupees instead of dollars. The evolving settlement patterns reflect geopolitical and economic realignments in the global oil market.
notes.now: Current energy market dynamics show fossil fuel prices with natural gas as the most volatile, contrasting with more stable oil and gasoline prices. Meanwhile, the economic competitiveness of renewables like solar and onshore wind is evidenced by their lower levelised costs compared to new fossil fuel generation. Concurrently, the monetary landscape of oil trade is subtly shifting away from exclusive dollar dominance, influenced by geopolitical developments and payment currency diversification.
